coredata.dc:title: A computational study on the novel defects of graphene quantum dot as a promising anode material for sodium ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Daryabari, Shabnam
Mansouri, Sakineh
Beheshtian, Javad
Karimkhani, Mehrnoosh
coredata.dc:description: The existence of defects in the graphene quantum dots can alter their electronic properties. In this study, the interaction of sodium ion/atom with defected graphene quantum dots (dGQDs) of F3.Gr, F3H3.Gr, N3.Gr and N3H3.Gr as the anode material for the sodium ion batteries was investigated using density functional theory calculations. The amount of charge transfer from Na atom to F3H3.Gr and from F3.Gr to Na ion is about 0.23 and 0.94|e|, respectively. In the complex of Na atom with dGQDs as an n-type-adsorbed dGQD, the Fermi level shifts towards the higher energy and it can be facilitated charge transfer from Na atom to dGQDs. The estimated electrical conductivity for the complexes of Na atom with F3.Gr and F3H3.Gr is 6.51 × 10−13 and 5.26 × 10−12, respectively. This can result in a favorable condition in the processes of battery charge and discharge. The cell voltage belonging to F- doped defects or N- doped defects is nearly 1.54–1.58 V or 0.58–0.61 V. It is concluded that the F- doped defects are more promising anode material for sodium ion batteries compared to N- doped defects because of their lower recovery time of adsorption, higher voltage, higher reactivity and better charge transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ion batteries
Defected graphene quantum dots
Density functional theory
Electronic properties

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties and the geometric structure changes of the dGQDs
Properties and the geometric structure changes of the Na+ on the defected GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Sakineh Mansouri would like gratitude to the Central Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran for their support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: NBO charge distribution on the edge atoms in optimized structures of F3-Gr (A), N3-Gr (B), F3H3-Gr (C) and N3H3-Gr (D). The number inserted in the center of the ring is the sum of the charges of edge atoms.

NBO charge distribution on the edge atoms in optimized structures with atom/ion of Na: Na@N3H3.Gr (A), Na+@N3H3.Gr (B), Na@F3H3.Gr (C), Na+@F3H3.Gr (D), Na@N3.Gr (E), Na+@N3.Gr (F), Na@F3.Gr(G). Na+@F3.Gr(H).
Summary diagram of theoretical study on the Na@dGQDs as an anode material: Na@F3H3.Gr (A), Na@N3H3.Gr (B), Na@N3.Gr (C), Na@F3.Gr (D).
The energies of HOMO, LUMO, HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg), fermi level and the cohesive energy.

The adsorption energies (Eads ) of the F and N-doped defected graphene with atom/ion of Na, Energies of HOMO, LUMO and HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg), ΔEg indicates the change of Eg after the atom/ion of Na adsorption and Q indicates the NBO charge transfer amount from the defected graphene to Na+ ion or from Na atom to defected graphene.

head.ce:title: A computational study on the novel defects of graphene quantum dot as a promising anode material for sodium ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Central Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Central Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The existence of defects in the graphene quantum dots can alter their electronic properties. In this study, the interaction of sodium ion/atom with defected graphene quantum dots (dGQDs) of F3.Gr, F3H3.Gr, N3.Gr and N3H3.Gr as the anode material for the sodium ion batteries was investigated using density functional theory calculations. The amount of charge transfer from Na atom to F3H3.Gr and from F3.Gr to Na ion is about 0.23 and 0.94|e|, respectively. In the complex of Na atom with dGQDs as an n-type-adsorbed dGQD, the Fermi level shifts towards the higher energy and it can be facilitated charge transfer from Na atom to dGQDs. The estimated electrical conductivity for the complexes of Na atom with F3.Gr and F3H3.Gr is 6.51 × 10−13 and 5.26 × 10−12, respectively. This can result in a favorable condition in the processes of battery charge and discharge. The cell voltage belonging to F- doped defects or N- doped defects is nearly 1.54–1.58 V or 0.58–0.61 V. It is concluded that the F- doped defects are more promising anode material for sodium ion batteries compared to N- doped defects because of their lower recovery time of adsorption, higher voltage, higher reactivity and better charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique physicochemical properties of graphene, notably its exceptionally high surface area, lack of band gap, electron mobility, thermal conductivity, and mechanical strength can lead to the eradication of environmental problems. In recent times, graphene-based nanomaterial has employed a lot of attention from researchers and experimentalists because of its potential applications in nanoscience, tissue engineering, tooth-whitening, membranes for bone regeneration, condensed matter physics, and spintronic data storage [1–3]. The graphene is an excellent candidate for use in supercapacitors (for energy storage) and can act as either a reducing agent or an oxidizing agent. So, graphene and its derivatives can be used as the electrode material in new battery technologies [4].

Recently, rechargeable lithium ion batteries (LIBs) technology has been considerably advanced, and widely used in several electronic devices, light vehicles, and portable power. Although the LIBs show outstanding performance, they suffer from a high cost, low lifetime, low-temperature performance, and safety problems [5–7]. Due to the variety and high usage of rechargeable batteries, battery production can certainly be expected to increase dramatically in the coming decades. Therefore, Sodium -ion batteries (NIBs) maybe a replacement for LIBs in near future. Sodium (Na) as a monovalent ion has a similar tendency to lose electrons which makes it a good anode material, and is therefore a potential alternative to lithium-based batteries. This element is low cost, nontoxicity, and truly earth-abundant and ubiquitous around the globe [8,9]. The atomic weight and ionic radius size of Na-ion are not very large and can be intercalated into electrode materials [10–12]. Theoretical studies showed the potential application of B12N12 nanoclusters as an anode in Na-ion batteries and that encapsulation of different halides into BN cage significantly increases the cell voltage in CNT-based ion batteries Choi et al. showed that [13]. The high transparent character of graphene suggests that many applications of graphene can be expected in other fields. Also, Xu et al. prepared metal graphene composites for fuel cells and found that the graphene resulted in improved cell performance [14]. Graphene-based materials have also been used as sorbent for environmental decontamination, as electrode materials for contaminant monitoring or removal and as building blocks for desalination membranes. The recent developments show the cutting graphene sheets into small pieces can obtain graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which exhibit many interesting properties [15]. The spatial confinement of electronic states is an efficient idea to tuning the properties of graphene and creating a useful band gap that the adsorption of certain ions or molecules can modify the size of the band gap [16,17]. Thus to design suitable graphene, it is necessary to develop a method for chemical doping or production of porous graphene with a proper diameter [ [1,18–22]]. In addition, the edge carbon atoms of pores in porous graphene, which have high chemical activity, should be passivated. A method in the molecular design strategy is based on extending the electrode material's π-conjugated system to achieve unique electrical, electronic, mechanical and other outstanding properties. Despite many studies and the advancement in experimental technologies in the preparation of porous graphene, understanding of the structure-property relationship remains unclear.

At the present time, an important part of the research is on the use of GQDs in ion batteries. The functionality of GQDs can improve their properties such as solubility, biocompatibility, and water stability, which results in more extensive applications of GQDs [23]. Theoretically, Maarouf et al. investigate simulation of cation and anion doped nonporous graphene. Their results suggested that the chemical doping of passivated graphene nanoporous is stable and could be controlled [24]. in 2011 cui et al., showed N-doped graphene nanosheets were a promising candidate for anode materials [25]. To date, the theoretical studies of graphene nanoporous are mostly at the electronic level to explain the ion binding between alkali metal ions [26]. However, at the molecular level, ion transport behaviors through the nanoporous have not yet been clearly understood.

The understanding of ion transport behaviors is essential in applications such as sensing and energy storage [27–30]. Moreover, modification of graphene has been shown use in living cells without any toxic effects, in contrary to hydrophobic pristine graphene, and extremely is used for medical applications [31,32]. This study introduces the defected graphene quantum dots (dGQDs) so that they can be used for many applications: battery, membrane technology and sensing platform. We report properties of four defects of graphene which doped by N, H, and F using first principles calculations and then investigate the interaction and adsorption of Na ion/atom on the defected graphene. The two main objectives of this study are: 1. introducing the novel defects of graphene. 2. investigation of their potential application as anode materials to achieve the high voltage and performance of Na ion batteries.

All DFT calculations were performed using the GAMESS program [33–35]. The Becke three-parameter Lee-Yang-Parr hybrid)B3LYP) [36,37] was also utilized to compute optimized geometries and calculate the energetic, electronic, and natural bond orbital (NBO) charge transfer [37,38] functional method and 6-31G(d) basis set which is confirmed to be suitable for calculating electronic and structural properties of dGQD systems [19,20]. We applied the dispersion term of Grimme “D” to predict the weak interaction appropriately [39]. The EBSSE is the basis set superposition error energy calculated by the counterpoise approach [40]. In this study, because of the importance of Na ion as mentioned in the introduction, the interaction and adsorption of Na ion and Na atom were investigated on the defected graphene. Structural models of the dGQDs are C87H27N3(N3H3-Gr), C84H24N3(N3-Gr), C87H27F3(F3H3-Gr), and C87H24F3(F3-Gr). Since the electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots depend strongly on their shape, size, pore, and edge disorder, large GQD was chosen to minimize the effect of the edge on the adsorption location, and also are not poisoned more quickly in experience. Initially put atom/ion Na in center of defected graphene and then optimized. The adsorption energy between this atom/ion and defected graphene was analyzed and compared. The adsorption energy (Ead) of Na/Na+ with dGQD (Na/Na+@dGQD) was calculated using the following equation [41–44]:
(1)
E ad
= E
(
Na +
/ Na @ dGQD
)
−
E
( dGQDs )
– E
(
Na +
/ Na
)
+
E BSSE
Where E(
dGQDs
) and E(Na/Na+) refers to the total energy of structural models and Na/Na+, respectively, and E (Na/Na+@
dGQD
) refers to the total energy each structural model with a Na atom or Na ion putted in its center.

Here, in order to compare the anode materials, we calculated the cohesive energy (Ecoh) as follows [33]:
(2)
E coh
=
E
(
Na +
/ Na @ dGQD
)
– E
(
Na +
/ Na
)
– 3
E
F / N
–
m E H
–
n E C
4 + n + m
where E Na/Na+@dGQD denotes the total energy of each structural model with an atom or ion Na, E Na/Na+ represents the energy of an isolated Na atom or Na+ ion, and EH or EC designate the energy of isolated hydrogen or carbon atom. Also, n and m are the numbers of C and H atoms, respectively.

HOMO-LUMO energy gap (Eg) of nano graphene and its complexes are defined as:
(3)
E g
=
E LUMO
−
E HOMO
The HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) is the highest-energy orbital with one or two electrons. The LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) is the lowest-energy orbital with no electrons.

The change of Eg as an index of sensitivity or chemical activity is obtained by:
(4)
Δ
E g
=
(
E
g 2
−
E
g 1
E
g 1
)
× 100
The larger HOMO-LUMO gap always refer to higher kinetic stability or lower chemical activity [45]. The GaussSum program was applied to draw the DOS schemes [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties and the geometric structure changes of the dGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: An efficient pattern to induce a band-gap in graphene is to design superstructures of graphene quantum dots [47]. We used precise manipulation of the defect and doping on the perfect graphene quantum dots (Gr) with D6h symmetry has 120 atoms and 37 rings of carbon as shown in Fig. S1. The bond length and energy gap value (Eg) of Gr are 1.42 Å, and 3.43 eV, so that the HOMO and LUMO energies are about −5.56 eV, and −2.13 eV, respectively. The calculated value is in agreement with the values reported in previous studies [48–51]. In the first type defect, C87H27F3 (F3H3-Gr) the central carbon ring (C6) of Gr was deleted, three carbon atoms of edge were replaced by F atoms, and three carbons were saturated with H atoms), see Fig. 1 C. The other nanoporous defected graphene (dGQDs) are, N3-Gr, N3H3-Gr, and F3-Gr that the middle piece of their optimized structures were shown in Fig. 1.

The Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) charge distribution of dGQDs on the atoms in the edge also has shown Fig. 1. The sum of the charges of edge atoms (the net charge) display in the center of rings. The results of quantum-chemical calculations of structural models are listed in Table 1 . Since Ecoh is a physical quantity that is useful for a measure of static stability, we calculate Ecoh for dGQDs. According to Ecoh, N-doped defects have lower static stability compared to F- doped defects. It can be due to the high polarizability of the N atom with respect to F atom (about two times higher than that of F atom) [52] which can provide the better transfer of charge from carbon to nitrogen, and the strong local charge concentrated at nitrogen sites. The high band gap energy of F- doped defects confirms their stability with respect to N- doped defects. We found that Eg dGQDs are in the visible region, so these defects can be used in many applications. The increase of the band gap energy and inherent stability of F- doped nanoporous defects can be useful for solar cell applications and high temperature electronics. It should be noted that the reduction of Eg of dGQDs with respect to the perfect GQD is consistent with the recent experimental and theoretical data [53,54].

By considering a charged ring (see Fig. 1) and its driving force, which is created from the partial charge of edge atoms belonging to the dGQDs, these nanoporous defects can have a positively or negatively charged edges which make these more reactive for interaction with negative or positive ions. Furthermore, positively or negatively charged edge in the dGQD sheet enlarges the interlayer spacing which can facilitate the absorption, intercalation and penetration of ions and molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties and the geometric structure changes of the Na+ on the defected GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption energy of atoms and ions with dGQDs was calculated by equation (1). The results are listed in Table 2 . Taking a closer look at Table 2 and Fig. 1 show the net charge of the edge atoms (the ring) and repulsion of positive partial charge hydrogen atoms in the edge are important factors in the adsorption energy. The results show the F3. Gr and N3. Gr defected graphene can act as a good sink for the attraction of Na ion or atom and improved Na+ storage properties. This is in agreement with recent work [11], which shows the more strengthening the interaction of the Na+ with the surface of the cage. As mentioned in the introduction, Cation–π interactions play an important role in the adsorption energy [10].

It seems high negatively charged edges increase the uptake and penetration of ions and atoms to defected graphene surface. Our finding shows the net charge and the adsorption energy of F3. Gr and N3. Gr are −0.99 and −0.312 and −3.79 and −3.23 eV, respectively, and the more negative net charge leads to more adsorption energy. The N3H3-Gr has smaller adsorption energy because of the repulsion of between positive partial charge hydrogen atoms of edge and the partial charge of atom/ion Na. The other nanoporous defects show the negative adsorption energy with the Na atom, which is a factor desirable for anode material and can prevent formation clustering of Na atom. According to previous articles [55,56], although high adsorption can lead to theoretical specific capacity, it will also affect the migration ability in the battery due to the trap effect of defects. Therefore, the medium adsorption energy is beneficial to the buttery properties. Also, the positively charged edge of F3H3-Gr reduces its tendency to adsorption of atom/ion Na due to repulsion force, so that it shows positive adsorption energy whit Na atom. Xiazi Song believes that this positive adsorption is due to the change in the adsorption mechanism from ionic to nonionic [57]. It is noteworthy that although F3H3-Gr has a positive adsorption energy value with the Na atom which is not desirable, it have the highest charge transfer about 0.94 |e| to the Na atom. It shows that the Na atom can be intercalated as a good gust into F3H3-Gr as a host, and the energy of intercalation can supply by charge transfer between the guest and the host. Table 2 also shows Na@F3.Gr and Na@F3H3.Gr have the lowest HOMO–LUMO gap which seems to be proper candidates for use in NIBs as an anode material due to higher chemical reactivity.

My molecular design strategy is based on extending π-conjugated edges which improves the charge transport and stabilizes the charged/discharged states so that they can better tolerate the fast insertion/extraction of ions. This strategy for creating defects can be very useful and practical in the creation of electrode materials and selective pores in graphene quantum dots to allow adsorption or the separation of atom, molecule, or ion particle. We find that due to the inhomogeneity of electron configuration on the edges, the nature of defects and the net charge of edge can affect the adsorption energy.

Based on the results in Table 2 and 3, atom/ion of Na meaningfully disturb the electronic properties of the dGQDs, but in different trends. Fermi level is the term used to describe the top of the collection of electron energy levels at absolute zero temperature. In the dGQDs with an adsorbed Na+ ion, as shown in Table 3 , the Fermi level shifts towards the lower energy level, for example, the Fermi level of F3. Gr shifts from −3.42 to −5.65 eV in Na+@F3.Gr. The Fermi level of Na+ is very low, −24.89eV, therefore, we can be regarded as an acceptor and the charge transfer is from the graphene defects to Na+ ion. This indicates Na+@F3.Gr would be regarded as a p-type-adsorbed defected graphene quantum dot. The NBO charge analysis indicates that a charge about 0.23 |e| transferred from F3. Gr to the Na cation. This can be attributed to the fact that Na cation prefers to gain some electrons from the π-conjugated edge atoms. In contrast, in the case Na@F3.Gr, the Fermi level shifts to −2.7 eV, and would be regarded as an n-type-adsorbed defected graphene quantum dot. Na atom has higher Fermi level (−2.19 eV), so can be regarded as a donor and the charge (about 0.766 |e|) transfer from Na atom to F3.Gr. The result of other defects is similar to that in F- doped defects which listed in Table 2 and 3 These results, are in agreement with the literature about F- doping [58].

NBO charge distribution on the edge atoms in optimized structures of the F-doped and N-doped defected graphene with atom/ion of Na has shown in Fig. 2 . This figure confirms the charge transfer from the π-conjugated edge atoms of dGQDs to atom/ion Na and vice versa. Also, based on the density of states’ (DOS) plots, the changes in energy level due to the HOMO/LUMO interaction, are seen in Fig. S2 (supplementary materials). A comparison of the images of Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 show that the HOMO/LUMO interaction of Na+ with the F3. Gr should be stronger than that of the Na atom. The analysis of DOS belonging to Na atom and Na+ indicates that the energy of their orbital (3s) is about −4.2 and −6.56 eV, respectively. By the adsorption of Na+ ion on the defected graphene quantum dot, both of the levels of HOMO and LUMO changes significantly shifts to lower energies. For example, in F3.Gr, the HOMO level moves from −5.02 to −7.41 eV in Na+@F3.Gr that can be due to interaction HOMO/LUMO belonging to the Na ion and of F3. Gr. The analysis of orbitals belonging to Na ion is according to below graph indicates that the energy level of LUMO is about −6.55eV which is below the HOMO level of F3.Gr (that is −5.02 eV). So HOMO/LUMO interaction provide charge transfer between the delocalized π orbital of F3.Gr and the unoccupied orbital of the Na ion and both of the levels of HOMO and LUMO changes significantly shifts to lower energies. Also the energy of 3p orbitals belonging to Na ion with no electrons are about −3.89 eV which become the new LUMO for the Na+@F3.Gr, and LUMO level moves from −1.82 to −3.89 eV. Unlike the Na+, by the adsorption of Na atom, the HOMO level changes from −5.02 to −3.43 in the Na@F3.Gr (see Tables 1 and 2), but the level of LUMO does not change meaningfully compared to the HOMO level. It significantly decreases HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg) to 1.45 eV in the Na@F3.Gr and leads to a better charge transfer from atom to the graphene. The greater drop at % ΔEg value are related to Na@dGQDs, in the range of −34.47 to −61.83 eV, which are consistent with high charge transfer values Q|e| (from 0.94 to 0.76 |e|) in Table 2. The pattern of the change of the electronic properties in the other defected graphene is similar to that in the Na@F3.Gr as shown in Table 2.

For further investigating the electronic properties, we applied the following equation formula for calculation of the estimated electronic conductivity (σ) which is a dimensionless quantity [59].
(5)
σ
α
exp
(
−
E g
/ 2
k B
T
)
Where kB is the Boltzmann's constant and T is the temperature. The electrical conductivity exponentially increases with decreasing Eg. The F- doped defects have higher electronic conductivity that can result in a favorable condition in the processes of battery charge and discharge due to the low amount of ohmic heat produced [60,61]. The estimated electrical conductivity of Na@F3.Gr or Na@F3H3.Gr is 6.51 × 10−13 or 5.26 × 10−12. Therefore, the results show F-doped dGQDs can be as better anode materials. It seems that the high HOMO/LUMO interaction of the F-doped dGQDs with Na atom can facilitate the passage of electric current and caused high electrical conductivity. Huang et al. also showed that the F doped graphene exhibited better electrochemical performance [62,63]. Also, the HOMO/LUMO profile in Fig. S3 &S4 clearly show the HOMO of Na+@F3.Gr largely located on the edge atoms which confirm higher Cation–π interaction Na ion with the polarizable π electron cloud of the F3.Gr. These profiles also show that the π interaction in the Na@F3 is lower than that the Na+@F3. Although it is not really possible to calculate some battery properties in this theoretical study, we calculate recovery time (
τ
) and cohesive energy (Ecoh) by the following equation (6) and equation (2), respectively, to estimate battery life and reactivity anode materials.
(6)
τ =
v 0
− 1
exp
( −
E ads
k B
T
)
Where v0 is the attempt frequency (which is set to 1013 Hz),
k B
is the Boltzmann's constant, and T is the temperature. Table 3 shows the lowest recovery time (nearly 1.23 × 10−7 s) is belong to Na+@F3H3.Gr. This can be useful in the fast insertion/extraction and diffusion of the Na ions in experience due to the fast refreshing surface of anode material which can enhance battery capacity. Furthermore, cohesive energy is a physical quantity that is useful for a measure of static stability. Static stability is the ability of a structure to return to a steady state after small mode disturbances [64]. For The prediction of the energy of batteries in terms of Ecoh according to the paper of Klaus Schmidt-Roh [65], we are calculated cohesive energy and listed it in Table 3. The results show F- doped defects have lower static stability compared to N- doped defects which can have spatiotemporal variations or higher reactivity in the surface [66]. As an example, Ecoh in complex Na+@F3H3.Gr is −7.33 eV and it can be seen that the difference in Ecoh between the N- doped defects and F- doped defects is about 0.2–0.3 eV. It seems that Na ions can easily diffuse out of F- doped defects. In the Na@F3H3.Gr, the Ecoh is about 0.35 eV lower than that of Na@N3H3.Gr, it could probably facilitate the oxidation process at the F- doped defects. Since in the oxidation process, atom transfer out of the anode material against the bonding forces associated with the cohesive energy, the F- doped defects can be a promising candidate for anode materials.

Batteries need to have high voltage because they are valued as devices that store chemical energy and convert it into electrical energy. Considering the Na-π/Na+-π interactions as the main factor of electron transfer and a dGQD as anode for NIBs, one can write the two half-reactions taking place at the two electrodes as:
Na@ dGQD↔ Na+ @ dGQD + e− (in anode).

Na+ + e− ↔ Na (in cathode) Therefore, the total reaction is as follows:
(7) Na+ + Na@ dGQD↔ Na+@ dGQD + Na

Since the electrical energy released is equal to the reduction in Gibbs energy, which is the hallmark of a spontaneous process, The Nernst expression can be used to calculate the cell voltage (E cell) as in the followings [67]:
(8)
E cell
=
-
Δ
G cell
/nF
(9)
Δ
G cell
=
Δ
U cell
+ P Δ
V cell
- T Δ
S cell
Where n, ΔGcell, and F are a number of electrons transferred in the balanced equation, Gibbs free energy change, and Faraday's constant, respectively. As presented in Eq. (9), the higher negative value of ΔUcell generates a larger cell voltage.

Previously, it has been demonstrated the contribution of entropy and volume changes in Eq. (9) is negligible in calculation of cell voltage because their effect on the cell voltage is smaller than 0.01 V [68,69]. Thus, the ΔGcell becomes equal to the ΔUcell as follows: ΔGcell = ΔUcell = U (Na) +U (Na+@dGQD)–U (Na+) – U (Na @dGQD) (10)

The maximum electrical work is equivalent to the reduction of Gibbs energy, which is indicative of the spontaneous process. We can see that (Na+ – π) interaction is favorable for the internal energy (U) based on Nernst equation and is beneficial to the cell voltage. The values of ΔUcell and cell voltage of F3.Gr are calculated as nearly −148.10 kJ/mol and 1.54V, respectively. These parameters are about −152.78 kJ/mol and 1.58V for F3H3.Gr, whereas N-doped dGQDs suffer from a low cell voltage (nearly 0.6 V) due to a lower negative value of ΔGcell (about 95 kJ/mol). Our dates show the calculated cell voltages are in agreement with the %ΔEg values. Also Table 3 shows a higher value of cohesive energy and recovery time of N-doped defects with Na ion can be a risk factor which, in practice, can reduce the battery capacity.

The studies show there are several factors involved in having a good ion battery. The Gibbs free energy is the first and most important factor. None of the factors of the charge transfer, the gap energy, and the electrical conductivity alone determine the cell voltage and interpretation just based on the charge transfer is not corrected. We found that the cell voltage generated by the studied dGQDs has the following order: F3H3. Gr > F3. Gr > N3H3. Gr > N3. Gr. As a summary of my theoretical study based on the cell voltage has shown in Fig. 3 . We think F-doped dGQDs, especially F3H3. Gr, act as a proper material for use in NIBs as an anode material due to the fact that Na@F3H3.Gr and Na+@F3H3.Gr have proper recovery time, charge transfer, and adsorption energy. It can be concluded that our proposed models can be more suitable anodic materials due to the generation of high cell voltage, compared to the AlN nanotube and nanosheets anodes studied by Weichi et al. [57, 70].

We report a comparative study of four defects of graphene which doped by N, H, and F using first principle calculations. Our results show that there are several factors involved in having a good ion battery and the interpretation just based on the charge transfer is not corrected. The nature of defects and the net charge of edge can affect the adsorption energy. The cell voltage belonging to F- doped defects is more than N- doped defects. It seems that the high HOMO/LUMO interaction of the F-doped dGQDs with the Na atom can facilitate the passage of electric current and caused high electrical conductivity. The estimated electrical conductivity of Na@F3.Gr and Na@F3H3.Gr are 6.51 × 10−13 and 5.26 × 10−12 that can result in a favorable condition in the processes of battery charge and discharge due to the low amount of ohmic heat.
The above studies will guide the application of F-doped dGQDs in metal ion batteries and the understanding of the effects of defects on electronic and structural properties.

Shabnam Daryabari: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Software. Sakineh Mansouri: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Javad Beheshtian: Methodology, Software, Visualization. Mehrnoosh Karimkhani: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Sakineh Mansouri would like gratitude to the Central Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran for their support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2021.124484.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: AlN nanotubes and nanosheets as anode material for K-ion batteries: DFT studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Weichi, L. Haiyang, Z. Xuejing, Z. Wenming, S. Ebrahimi, AlN nanotubes and nanosheets as anode material for K-ion batteries: DFT studies, Phys. Lett, (2020) 126396, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physleta.2020.126396.
